definition: Flat 40% federal tax, on top of estate/gift tax, on transfers to grandchildren or anyone 37.5+ years younger; separate $15M exemption (2026), not portable between spouses. See the GST Explainer tab.
Period during which transferred property could still be pulled back into the transferor's estate; GST exemption cannot be effectively allocated until it ends. See the ETIP Explainer tab.
Minimum IRS interest rate for intra-family loans and installment sales (IDGT notes). July 2026 annual: 4.00% short-term / 4.35% mid-term / 4.98% long-term.

term: IRS assumed interest rate used to value trust (IRS Section 7520 Rate)
definition: Monthly IRS rate (120% of the mid-term AFR) used to value annuities, life estates, and remainders — the hurdle for GRATs and CLATs, the discount driver for QPRTs and CRTs. July 2026: 5.2%.

July 2025 law that made the enlarged exemptions permanent: $15M estate/gift and GST exemption per person from 2026, indexed thereafter; also raised the QSBS cap to $15M for stock issued after July 4, 2025.
Exclusion of capital gain on qualifying C-corporation stock: the greater of $10M ($15M for stock issued after July 4, 2025) or 10x basis, per taxpayer. California does not conform.
Gifting qualifying shares to separate NON-grantor trusts before a sale so each trust claims its own Section 1202 cap. Grantor trusts (SLATs, GRATs, standard IDGTs) do not stack.
Trust whose income is taxed to the grantor. The grantor's tax payments are an extra, gift-tax-free transfer to the trust — a feature, not a bug.
Trust that is its own income taxpayer — the prerequisite for QSBS stacking and for state-income-tax planning.
Irrevocable trust deliberately drafted as a grantor trust for income tax but outside the estate for estate tax — the chassis for installment-sale freezes.
The ~10% equity gift made to an IDGT before an installment sale so the trust has real capital and the note is respected as debt.
Grantor's right to exchange assets of equivalent value with the trust — the standard grantor-trust trigger and the main tool for pulling low-basis assets back before death for the step-up.
Reset of an asset's income-tax basis to fair market value at death, erasing built-in gain. Assets given away during life keep carryover basis — the price of most transfer strategies.
A surviving spouse's ability to use the deceased spouse's unused FEDERAL exemption (requires a timely Form 706 election). Does not apply to the GST exemption, and New York has no portability at all.

A beneficiary's short-term right to withdraw a gift to a trust, which converts the gift into a present interest qualifying for the $19,000 annual exclusion — the funding engine of ILITs. The notices must actually be sent and kept.

Court doctrine that uncrosses mirror-image trusts created by spouses for each other, pulling both back into the estates. The defense: make the two SLATs genuinely different in terms, assets, trustees, and timing.
Setting the retained annuity (GRAT) or charitable annuity (CLAT) so the taxable remainder gift is approximately $0 — the structure consumes essentially no exemption.
Rule for gifts of carried interest / GP stakes: transfer a proportionate slice of the ENTIRE interest (carry and capital together) or the deemed-gift rules can tax far more than was given.
The retained-interest pull-back: keep enjoyment or control of transferred property (living rent-free in the QPRT house, treating trust assets as your own) and it lands back in your estate.
Charitable Remainder Unitrust (payout = fixed % of annually revalued assets; can accept additions) versus Annuity Trust (fixed dollar payout; no additions). The CRUT is the default choice.
Charitable Lead Annuity Trust (fixed payment to charity; efficient for zeroing out but bad for GST) versus Unitrust (percentage payment; can lock GST allocation at funding).
Charitable account giving an immediate deduction with grant timing flexibility — a common lead beneficiary for CLATs and remainder beneficiary for CRTs.
Marital trust: all income to the surviving spouse for life, remainder controlled by the first spouse's plan; marital deduction via election on Form 706. See Situational Trusts tab.
Election treating QTIP assets as still the first spouse's for GST purposes — the tool that keeps a first-to-die spouse's GST exemption from being wasted.
Trust funded at the first spouse's death to capture that spouse's exemption — essential in New York, where the state exclusion is not portable. See Situational Trusts tab.
The required vehicle for the marital deduction when the surviving spouse is not a U.S. citizen. See Situational Trusts tab.
Trust supporting a disabled beneficiary without destroying means-tested benefits; third-party (no payback) versus first-party (Medicaid payback). See Situational Trusts tab.

Self-settled trust under NV/SD/AK/DE law protecting assets while the grantor remains a beneficiary — contested enforceability, and California courts do not respect it for CA residents. See Situational Trusts tab.
Parent-seeded trust the beneficiary is deemed to own under Section 678, letting the beneficiary sell assets to it — aggressive, thinly supported, audit-sensitive. See Situational Trusts tab.
State-income-tax avoidance trust. Dead for California residents (taxed as grantor trusts since 2023) and New York residents (2014).
California's mechanism for taxing previously accumulated trust income when it is finally distributed to a California-resident beneficiary.

2021 property-tax rules that trigger reassessment on most parent-child transfers of real estate — including a QPRT residence at term end — unless a child occupies it as a primary residence within the exclusion limits.
Doctrine taxing the person who earned the gain even after a transfer — the reason QSBS gifts and CRT funding must happen BEFORE a binding sale agreement exists.
Pouring an existing trust's assets into a new trust with better terms — the main repair tool for old or inflexible irrevocable trusts.
Health, Education, Maintenance, Support — the distribution standard that lets a beneficiary (or beneficiary-trustee) receive support without causing estate inclusion.
